mainEntity.name: How much does a Defender engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Defender engine typically costs £2,500–£9,500 supply only, depending on generation and engine [EM-QUOTE]. Classic Tdi units sit at £2,500–£4,500; L663 six-cylinder diesels at £6,500–£9,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,900 depending on labour rates. Use our Defender calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Defender engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the Classic, the 300Tdi — mechanically injected, endlessly rebuildable, and demanding little beyond disciplined timing-belt changes. On the L663, the six-cylinder D300 is proving the most dependable unit in our data.

mainEntity.name: Which Defender engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.4 TDCi 'Puma' (2007–2011) carries the Classic's highest failure risk — injector seal failure can end in bottom-end damage. On the L663, early 2020–21 four-cylinder D200/D240 builds warrant a timing-chain check before purchase.

Yes — it holds our 🏆 Safest Buy ranking across the entire Land Rover range. The six-cylinder engines are proving robust, and with host values of £35,000–£90,000, even a worst-case engine replacement is comfortably economically viable [EM-QUOTE].

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Defender engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Almost always — uniquely among Land Rovers. L663 values and rising Classic values mean replacement cost rarely approaches our 60% scrap-threshold rule. Use the Ownership Decision Tool for your specific vehicle.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Defender engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Classic: stamped on the block (left side on Tdi/TD5) and on the chassis/VIN plate. L663: listed in the vehicle documentation and readable via the VIN — or tell us your variant (D250, P400 etc.) and we'll identify it.

mainEntity.name: Defender Classic vs L663 — which should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: Different vehicles for different lives. The Classic is a rebuildable working tool and appreciating asset; the L663 is a refined, safe long-term keeper.

mainEntity.name: TD5 or TDCi — which Classic Defender engine is better?

acceptedAnswer.text: The TD5 — a stronger long-term proposition with well-understood, inexpensive weak points (injector loom, early head castings). The TDCi's 6-speed box is nicer to drive, but the 2.4's injector and crank history demands full documentation.

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The Defender is the only Land Rover where engine replacement is financially safe at almost any spec — the question is never "is it worth it?", only "which engine do I have and what does it cost?"

generations.subHeadline: Two Defenders, two completely different ownership propositions. Select yours to explore engines, problems, and costs.

cards.title: Generation Cards (opinionated format per v2 spec)
cards.verdict: Mechanically honest and endlessly rebuildable — buy on engine choice, not on year. 300Tdi for simplicity, TD5 for usability, approach the 2.4 TDCi Puma with a full injector history.

cards.title: Defender L663
cards.verdict: The safest ownership proposition in the entire JLR range. High residuals mean replacement is always economically viable; the six-cylinder diesels are proving genuinely robust. Prefer 2021+ builds.

cards.title: The Classic vs L663 Question
cards.meta: Unlike any other Land Rover, the Defender's two generations aren't an evolution — they're different vehicles sharing a name. The Classic is a body-on-frame working tool whose engines can be rebuilt indefinitely at reasonable cost. The L663 is an aluminium-monocoque luxury 4x4 whose engines are modular Ingenium units — replaced, not rebuilt at the roadside. Your repair-vs-replace maths, your specialist network, and your parts supply are completely different depending on which side of 2016 your Defender sits.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: Confidence Score panel (per v2 spec — evidence-based ratings): How confident are these ratings? L663 Ingenium ratings: 92% confidence — based on 292 Defender-specific enquiries [EM-VERIFIED], cross-referenced against 1,150+ 204DTD enquiries across the JLR range, plus specialist rebuild reports. Classic engine ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — built on documented failure patterns and specialist interviews rather than proprietary volume data (per our data-integrity policy, we label rather than inflate). Data note (internal): Enquiry volumes from EM_Proprietary_Data_2025.txt [EM-VERIFIED]. Costs from Table A [EM-QUOTE]. Classic-era enquiry volumes not yet captured — flagged as pattern data, not EM-VERIFIED.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The failures Defender owners actually report — with symptoms, costs, and urgency levels.

problems.description: The Classic Puma's defining weakness. Injector seal failure washes bores with diesel; unchecked, it ends in bottom-end failure. £3,200–£5,500 recon [EM-QUOTE]. Affects 2007–2011 Classic.
Oil wicks up the injector loom into the ECU connector — rough running, misfires, apparent 'ECU failure'. Cheap fix caught early; expensive misdiagnosis if not.
Pre-2002 10P heads can crack between valves — coolant loss and white smoke. Later 15P heads largely resolved it.
The Ingenium diesel's known weak point, mostly on pre-2019-derived units. Cold-start rattle is the warning. Early L663 D200/D240 builds are the ones to check.
Short-journey L663 diesels can suffer fuel-in-oil dilution from interrupted DPF regens — accelerates wear if oil changes are stretched.
The one thing that kills an otherwise immortal engine. Belt and tensioner every 60k miles or 5 years — no exceptions.

urgencyKey.label: Low
urgencyKey.text: plan at next service Each card links to a dedicated failure page: symptoms, root cause, repair cost vs replacement cost, urgency, next steps.

marketIntelligence.h2: Defender Market Intelligence — What 292 UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising sharply as early L663s exit warranty

signals.data: 980 [EM-VERIFIED] — tracked as 306DT in source data; covers the Ford V6 era. AJ20D6 (L663) volume to be split out.

signals.demandTrend: ➡ Stable — values keep rebuilds viable indefinitely

insights: Interesting insight: Defender L663 owners are disproportionately pre-purchase researchers — a notable share of our 2025 Defender enquiries were inspection and due-diligence requests rather than failure reports, the inverse of the Discovery 4 pattern. That's what a reputation for reliability looks like in enquiry data.
The L663 is the only JLR model in our data where enquiry volume is rising while failure-driven enquiries stay flat — demand is growing faster than problems are.

Early D200/D240 (2020–21 builds) generate the majority of L663 engine-concern enquiries; six-cylinder D250/D300 cars barely register. (Editorial synthesis of failure-pattern data — sense-check with JLR specialist before publishing, per homepage data-note policy.)
marketIntelligence.liveEnquiryFeedNote: Live Enquiry Feed module embeds here — e.g. "Defender L663 (D300 / AJ20D6) • Aberdeen • Pre-purchase inspection • 7 hours ago" [EM-VERIFIED]. (Note: the source feed currently labels this entry "306DT" — correct the label at source.)

replacementCosts.h2: Defender Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a Defender engine typically takes 10–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [EM-QUOTE]. The Defender rule: With L663 values of £35,000–£90,000, even a £9,500 recon engine sits far below our 60% scrap-threshold rule — replacement is ✅ Safe at any spec. Classic Defenders behave the same way for the opposite reason: values have risen so far that a £4,500 recon 300Tdi is trivial against a £15,000–£40,000 vehicle. The Defender is the one Land Rover we never recommend scrapping over an engine.

engineEvolution.h2: How the Defender Engine Range Evolved — 1990 to Today

profiles.verdict: L663 110/130 only — genuinely practical, and the safest JLR to own long-term. Classic is a lifestyle choice, not family transport.
L663 D250 is excellent; avoid diesel L663s on short urban runs (oil-dilution risk). Classic Defenders make hard work of commuting.
Best in class. D300's 650Nm and 3,500kg capacity, or a 300Tdi that will tow forever at its own pace.
Counter-intuitive: strong values mean repairs are always worth it, but nothing about a Defender is cheap. A tidy TD5 is the value sweet spot.
Our Best Long-Term Keeper across the whole JLR range. Classic values rising; L663 residuals class-leading.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My Defender Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Cold-start rattle on an early L663? Injector fault on a Puma? Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data — with Defender already pre-selected. Primary CTA (large, gold): Launch the Defender Diagnostic Calculator → /calculator/?model=defender

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every cost range and enquiry volume comes from genuine 2025 UK enquiry data [EM-VERIFIED] — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: With the Defender, that's rare: it's the one Land Rover where replacement is almost always economically safe. When that changes for your case, we'll say so.
